steps.title: Track the main claim
steps.content: The passage first notes the common reputation of Smith's invisible hand metaphor: it is often treated as a broad model of people producing social benefits by pursuing self-interest. Then the passage undercuts that reputation by saying Smith used the metaphor only once in his economic writings, and only for a narrow point about mercantilism.

steps.title: Focus on later promotion
steps.content: The passage's key explanation is that twentieth-century economists elevated the metaphor into a major paradigm because they wanted an intellectual pedigree for their own views. That means the metaphor's fame comes less from its importance in Smith's work than from later economists' promotion of it.

steps.title: Avoid distorted readings
steps.content: Choice B focuses too narrowly on Smith's original use. Choice C adds that the metaphor is useful, which the passage never says. Choice D wrongly describes later economists as realizing the metaphor was a robust model. The answer is A.
